title: Fuel pump failure and testing — all EFI motorcycles
description: Electric fuel pumps on EFI motorcycles are intank or frame-mounted units that deliver fuel at 36-58 PSI depending on the system. The pump motor brushes wear, the impeller corrodes from ethanol-laden fuel, or the pump relay/connector fails from vibration. When the pump weakens, fuel pressure drops below the injector operating threshold and the engine starves under load or refuses to start entirely. This affects all EFI makes: Honda CBR/VFR series, Yamaha R-series and FZ models, Kawasaki ZX and Z series, Suzuki GSX-R and V-Strom, and Harley-Davidson Dyna/Softail/Touring with Delphi EFI. A failing pump often gives intermittent symptoms before total failure.
causes: Fuel pump motor brush wear from high mileage — typically 40,000-60,000 miles
Impeller corrosion from ethanol fuel sitting in tank during storage
Fuel pump relay failure or corroded relay socket pins
Wiring harness connector at the pump corroded from fuel vapor exposure
Fuel filter/strainer clogged restricting flow to pump inlet
fix_procedure: 1. Key-on test: turn ignition to ON (do not crank) and listen for a 2-3 second fuel pump prime hum from the tank area. No prime = pump, relay, or wiring fault. 2. Check the fuel pump relay first — swap with another identical relay in the fuse box (horn relay is often the same part number). If the pump primes with the swapped relay, replace the relay ($8-15). 3. Check fuel pump connector: unplug the pump connector (under seat or at tank) and check for 12V with key on. No voltage = trace wiring back to relay and ECU. 4. Fuel pressure test: connect a fuel pressure gauge to the fuel rail test port (Schrader valve). Key-on pressure should be 36-43 PSI for most Japanese EFI, 55-62 PSI for Harley Delphi systems. Below spec = weak pump. 5. Flow test: disconnect the fuel return line and key-on — the pump should deliver at least 750 mL/min (Japanese) or 1 L/min (Harley). Low flow with good pressure = partially clogged strainer. 6. Replace pump assembly: drop the tank, remove the fuel pump module. Most Japanese bikes use a flange-mount module; Harleys use an in-tank module accessed from the top of the tank. 7. Replace the inlet strainer/filter with the pump — a new pump with an old clogged strainer will fail prematurely. 8. Forum tip: fuel pump failure is the most common no-start on EFI bikes that have been sitting. The #1 mistake is assuming the pump is dead when it's actually the relay or connector. Always check relay and voltage FIRST — that's a 5-minute free diagnosis. The OEM pump for a 2003-2006 Honda CBR600RR is Honda P/N 16700-MEE-013 ($180-220); aftermarket pumps from Quantum and All Balls are $80-120 and work well. For Harley Delphi systems, the OEM pump module is $350+; Quantum QFS-HFP-382HD ($130) is the go-to aftermarket replacement. On Kawasaki ZX-6R/10R (2004+), the fuel pump connector is notorious for corroding — clean the pins with electrical contact cleaner and apply dielectric grease. Yamaha R1/R6 pumps are generally reliable to 50K+ miles, but the inlet strainer clogs around 25K — clean it during valve service.
parts_needed: Fuel pump assembly (OEM or quality aftermarket: Quantum, All Balls)
Fuel pressure gauge with Schrader adapter

title: Throttle Position Sensor (TPS) calibration and failure — erratic idle and hesitation
description: The TPS is a potentiometer mounted on the throttle body that tells the ECU the exact throttle plate angle. A worn or miscalibrated TPS sends erratic voltage signals, causing the ECU to miscalculate fuel delivery. Symptoms include erratic idle that hunts up and down, hesitation on tip-in, sudden power surges, and check engine lights. This is a universal EFI issue across Kawasaki ZX-6R/10R, Suzuki GSX-R600/750/1000, Honda CBR series, Yamaha YZF-R1/R6, and Harley-Davidson throttle-by-wire models. On older bikes with cable throttle, the TPS is adjustable; on newer throttle-by-wire bikes, it is self-calibrating but can still fail.
causes: TPS potentiometer wiper track worn from mileage — creates dead spots in signal
TPS mounting screws loose — sensor shifts position on throttle body
TPS base voltage out of specification from drift over time
Water intrusion into TPS connector causing intermittent signal dropout
Throttle-by-wire TPS internal failure requiring ECU adaptation reset
fix_procedure: 1. Read DTCs: P0120-P0123 indicate TPS circuit issues. P0121 = range/performance (most common). 2. Backprobe TPS connector with multimeter: with key on, engine off, measure voltage at the signal wire (typically the center pin). At closed throttle: should read 0.4-0.9V (varies by make). At wide-open throttle: should read 4.0-4.8V. 3. Slowly rotate the throttle from closed to open while watching voltage — it must increase smoothly and linearly. Any dropout, jump, or dead spot = worn TPS. 4. On adjustable TPS (most cable-throttle bikes): loosen the two mounting screws, connect a multimeter, and rotate the TPS body until closed-throttle voltage reads factory spec. Kawasaki ZX-6R/10R spec is 0.51-0.59V; Suzuki GSX-R is 0.45-0.55V; Honda CBR is 0.45-0.55V. Tighten screws without moving the sensor. 5. On throttle-by-wire bikes: TPS is non-adjustable. Replace the throttle body assembly or TPS unit, then perform ECU TPS relearn (typically: key on, wait 5 seconds, key off, wait 10 seconds, repeat 3 times). 6. Clear DTCs and test ride — verify smooth idle and clean tip-in response. 7. Forum tip: the TPS is the second most common cause of erratic idle on EFI bikes after vacuum leaks. The classic symptom is an idle that hunts between 1200-1800 RPM in a rhythmic cycle — that's the ECU chasing a bad TPS signal. On Kawasaki ZX-6R (2003-2006), the TPS connector is exposed to water spray from the front wheel — always check the connector before condemning the sensor. Suzuki GSX-R TPS units (P/N 13580-29G00, $45-65) are known to wear at 30,000+ miles. On Harley throttle-by-wire models (2008+ Touring, 2016+ Softail), the TPS is integrated into the throttle body — replacement is the entire twist grip assembly ($200-350). After any TPS replacement on any make, ALWAYS clear the ECU adaptive memory or the old fuel trims will fight the new sensor readings.
parts_needed: Small Phillips or JIS screwdriver (for TPS mounting screws)
OBD2/diagnostic tool for DTC clearing and TPS relearn

title: Intake Air Pressure (IAP/MAP) sensor failure — incorrect fuel trim and boost reference
description: The MAP (Manifold Absolute Pressure) or IAP (Intake Air Pressure) sensor measures intake manifold vacuum and reports it to the ECU for load-based fuel calculations. When the MAP sensor fails or its vacuum line cracks, the ECU loses its primary load signal and defaults to a fixed fuel map, causing rich running at idle, lean running at high load, poor fuel economy, and a check engine light. This affects all speed-density EFI systems: Suzuki GSX-R series, Kawasaki ZX and Z models, Honda CBR/CRF, Yamaha FZ/MT/R-series, and Harley-Davidson with Delphi or later systems.
causes: MAP sensor internal piezoresistive element failure from heat and vibration
Vacuum hose to MAP sensor cracked, kinked, or disconnected
MAP sensor connector corroded or pins bent from service
Vacuum hose routing too close to exhaust — heat degrades the hose
Carbon buildup in the vacuum port on the throttle body restricting signal
fix_procedure: 1. Read DTCs: P0105-P0108 indicate MAP circuit issues. P0106 (range/performance) is most common. 2. Inspect vacuum hose FIRST: trace the small rubber hose from the throttle body/intake manifold to the MAP sensor. Check for cracks, kinks, disconnection. Replace the hose if in doubt ($2 of vacuum hose). 3. Key-on engine-off test: with a scan tool, read MAP sensor voltage or kPa. At sea level with engine off, it should read approximately 100 kPa (atmospheric). At idle, it should drop to 25-35 kPa (high vacuum). 4. If readings are stuck at one value or erratic: unplug the sensor connector and check for 5V reference voltage and ground. No 5V = wiring issue back to ECU. 5. Apply vacuum to the sensor with a hand vacuum pump — voltage should drop smoothly and proportionally. Jerky or stuck readings = failed sensor. 6. Replace the MAP sensor (typically 2 bolts and a connector). Clear DTCs. 7. Forum tip: the MAP sensor vacuum hose is the #1 overlooked item on EFI motorcycles. It's a $0.50 piece of rubber that causes $500 worth of diagnostic headaches. On Suzuki GSX-R models (all years), the MAP sensor vacuum hose runs near the #1 exhaust header and cooks from heat — replace it every 2-3 years proactively. The OEM MAP sensor for GSX-R600/750/1000 is Suzuki P/N 18590-03H00 ($60-80); Denso 079800-7500 is the OEM supplier and can be cross-referenced for $40-50. On Kawasaki ZX models, the MAP sensor is mounted on the airbox — check for cracked airbox causing a false reading. Harley MAP sensors (Delphi) are $80-120 OEM; they fail more often than Japanese bikes because of the higher engine vibration on V-twins. On Yamaha R1/R6, the MAP sensor rarely fails but the vacuum hose T-connector that splits signal to multiple ports cracks at 30K+ miles.
parts_needed: OBD2/diagnostic tool for live data and DTC clearing

title: O2 sensor failure and elimination — closed-loop fueling issues
description: Oxygen sensors in EFI motorcycle exhaust systems measure residual oxygen to enable closed-loop fuel trim correction. When the O2 sensor fails, becomes contaminated, or is removed for an aftermarket exhaust, the ECU either runs in open-loop (fixed fuel map) or throws continuous fault codes. A lazy or failed O2 sensor causes rich or lean fuel trim drift, increased fuel consumption, failed emissions inspections, and persistent check engine lights. This affects Yamaha YZF-R1/R6 and FZ/MT series, Honda CBR and CRF-L models, Kawasaki Z and Versys series, Suzuki GSX-S and V-Strom, and all Harley-Davidson EFI models from 2006+.
causes: O2 sensor heater element failure — sensor takes too long to reach operating temperature
Sensor element contaminated by fuel additives, oil blow-by, or silicone sealant fumes
O2 sensor wiring harness damaged or connector corroded from exhaust heat
Aftermarket exhaust installed without O2 bung — sensor removed, open circuit fault
Sensor aged past useful life — response time degrades after 30,000-50,000 miles
fix_procedure: 1. Read DTCs: P0131-P0135 indicate O2 sensor circuit issues. P0134 (no activity detected) is the most common. 2. With a scan tool, read O2 sensor live data at idle: a functioning narrowband sensor should oscillate between 0.1V-0.9V at roughly 1-4 Hz. A stuck sensor (always high or always low) or a slow sensor (<1 Hz) is failed. 3. Check the O2 sensor heater circuit: measure resistance across the heater pins (typically 2-20 ohms depending on sensor). Infinite resistance = open heater. 4. Inspect sensor wiring for heat damage — the wires run near exhaust pipes and melt if not properly routed. 5. Replace the O2 sensor: use an O2 sensor socket (22mm with cutout for wires) and apply anti-seize to threads. Torque to 30-40 Nm. 6. For bikes with aftermarket exhaust without O2 bung: install an O2 eliminator/simulator ($15-25) or use an ECU flash/fuel controller to disable closed-loop O2 correction. 7. Clear DTCs and let the ECU re-adapt in closed loop (10-15 minutes of varied riding). 8. Forum tip: the O2 sensor is the most misunderstood component on EFI motorcycles. Mechanics blame it for everything, but a failed O2 sensor usually just means the ECU runs open-loop on its base fuel map — the bike will still run, just not optimally. On Yamaha R1/R6 (2004+), the O2 sensor is a Denso narrowband unit; the OEM replacement is $80-120, but a Denso universal (P/N 234-4209, $30-40) fits with minor wiring. On Harley-Davidson (2006+), the front and rear O2 sensors are different part numbers — the front is the primary and the rear is for catalyst monitoring. If you install a Vance & Hines or Screamin Eagle exhaust on a Harley, you need a fuel processor (FP3, $300) or ECU flash to eliminate the O2 sensor CEL. On Kawasaki Z900/Z650 and Suzuki GSX-S750, the O2 sensor rarely fails but the connector melts if the exhaust heat shield is removed — always keep the heat shield in place.
parts_needed: O2 sensor (OEM or Denso/Bosch universal with adapter)
O2 sensor socket (22mm with wire cutout)
O2 eliminator/simulator (if removing sensor for exhaust mod)

title: Engine Coolant Temperature (ECT) sensor failure — cold start enrichment and fan control
description: The ECT sensor is a negative temperature coefficient (NTC) thermistor that reports coolant temperature to the ECU. The ECU uses this signal for cold start enrichment, idle speed control, fan activation, and fuel trim correction. A failed ECT sensor causes hard cold starts (if the ECU thinks the engine is already warm), rich running in hot weather (if the ECU thinks the engine is cold), cooling fan not activating, and a temperature gauge that reads incorrectly. This is a cross-platform issue on Honda CBR and CRF-L models, Kawasaki Ninja and Z series, Suzuki V-Strom and GSX-S, Yamaha MT and Tenere, and Harley-Davidson liquid-cooled models (Street, LiveWire, Pan America).
causes: ECT thermistor element drifted out of specification from thermal cycling over years
ECT sensor connector corroded from coolant weeping past the sensor O-ring
Wiring harness to ECT sensor chafed against engine — intermittent short or open
ECT sensor installed with sealant instead of proper O-ring — sealant contaminates the thermistor
Coolant level low — sensor reads air temperature instead of coolant temperature
fix_procedure: 1. Read DTCs: P0115-P0118 indicate ECT circuit issues. P0116 (range/performance) means the sensor reads implausibly. 2. With engine cold (ambient temperature), read ECT sensor value on a scan tool — it should match ambient air temperature within 5 degrees C. If it reads -40C or 150C with engine cold, the sensor or wiring is failed. 3. Resistance check: unplug ECT connector and measure resistance. At 20C: approximately 2,000-3,000 ohms. At 80C: approximately 300-400 ohms. Out of range = replace sensor. 4. Check for 5V reference voltage at the connector with key on — no voltage means wiring issue to ECU. 5. Inspect the sensor O-ring and look for coolant weeping around the sensor body — this indicates a bad seal that will corrode the connector. 6. Replace ECT sensor: drain enough coolant to bring the level below the sensor, unscrew the sensor, apply new O-ring, install new sensor, refill coolant, bleed air. 7. Clear DTCs and verify the temperature reading tracks properly from cold start to operating temperature. 8. Forum tip: the ECT sensor is one of the cheapest sensors on the bike ($15-30) but causes the most frustrating cold-start problems. The classic complaint is 'my bike starts fine when it's warm but won't start on cold mornings' — that's a drifted ECT sensor telling the ECU the engine is at 60C when it's actually at 5C, so the ECU doesn't add cold-start enrichment. On Honda CBR600RR/1000RR, the ECT sensor is Denso type and the OEM part is $20 — replace it every 5 years proactively. On Kawasaki Ninja 650/Z650 (2017+), there are TWO temperature sensors — one for the ECU and one for the gauge/fan. Make sure you're testing the ECU sensor, not the gauge sender. On Suzuki V-Strom 650/1000, the ECT sensor connector is exposed to road spray and corrodes — apply dielectric grease after any service. Harley liquid-cooled models (Street 750, Pan America) use a standard M12 NTC sensor; Dorman P/N 904-7631 ($12) cross-references to the OEM unit.
parts_needed: ECT sensor O-ring or sealing washer
Coolant for top-off after sensor replacement

title: Fuel injector clogging and cleaning — spray pattern degradation
description: EFI motorcycle injectors are precision components that atomize fuel into a fine spray at 36-60 PSI. Over time, fuel varnish, ethanol deposits, and carbon buildup on the injector tip degrade the spray pattern from a fine mist to a stream or uneven cone. A partially clogged injector causes lean conditions on that cylinder, misfires at load, rough idle, and eventual backfiring. This is a universal issue on all EFI motorcycles: Kawasaki ZX-10R/ZX-6R, Suzuki GSX-R series, Yamaha R1/R6 and MT series, Honda CBR/CB series, and Harley-Davidson Delphi and later systems. Multi-cylinder engines show the symptom most clearly because one clogged injector creates an imbalance.
causes: Fuel varnish deposits from ethanol fuel evaporation on injector pintle
Carbon buildup on injector tip from intake heat and combustion blowback
Fuel filter bypassed or not serviced — debris reaches injector screens
Low-quality fuel with high sulfur content accelerating deposit formation
Bike stored with fuel in the system and no stabilizer — varnish forms inside injector
fix_procedure: 1. Diagnosis: read DTCs — P0201-P0204 indicate injector circuit faults (per cylinder). Live misfire counts on a scan tool will identify which cylinder has the clogged injector. 2. Fuel additive treatment (mild cases): run a full tank with Chevron Techron Concentrate ($8-12) or Yamaha Ring Free. This dissolves light varnish deposits without removal. 3. On-bike injector cleaning (moderate cases): connect an injector cleaning kit (OTC 7649 or similar) to the fuel rail, disable the fuel pump, and run the engine on cleaning solvent for 15-20 minutes. This pushes cleaning solution backward through the injector. 4. Off-bike ultrasonic cleaning (severe cases): remove injectors from the fuel rail, place in an ultrasonic cleaner with injector cleaning solution for 30-60 minutes while pulsing the injectors electrically. This is the most thorough method. 5. Spray pattern test: after cleaning, connect injectors to a bench tester and observe the spray pattern — it should be a fine, even cone. A stream, drip, or uneven pattern means the injector is beyond cleaning and must be replaced. 6. Replace injector O-rings (top and bottom) when reinstalling — old O-rings leak fuel or air. 7. Clear DTCs and test ride. 8. Forum tip: injector clogging is the EFI equivalent of the carb pilot jet clog — it's caused by the same thing (ethanol fuel varnish) and prevented the same way (don't let fuel sit). The Techron treatment genuinely works for mild cases — run it every 5,000 miles as preventive maintenance on any EFI motorcycle. For Kawasaki ZX-10R and ZX-6R, the injectors are Keihin top-feed units; aftermarket replacements from Delphi or Bosch can be adapted for $40-60 each vs $120+ OEM. On Suzuki GSX-R, the secondary injectors (on dual-injector models) clog first because they're used less frequently — the Techron treatment helps these the most. Harley injectors (Delphi) are robust but the fuel rail O-rings shrink from ethanol — replace them every 30K miles. Honda CBR injectors rarely clog if the fuel filter is serviced, but the filter is inside the tank and often forgotten — clean it at 20K miles.
parts_needed: Fuel injector cleaning solution (Techron Concentrate or equivalent)
Injector O-ring kit (top and bottom seals, per injector)

title: Idle Speed Control (ISC/IACV) valve carbon buildup — erratic or high idle
description: The ISC (Idle Speed Control) or IACV (Idle Air Control Valve) is a motorized valve that bypasses the throttle plate to regulate airflow at idle. Carbon and oil vapor from crankcase ventilation coat the valve and its bore, causing it to stick open (high idle), stick closed (stalling), or hunt between positions (surging idle). This is extremely common on Yamaha FZ/MT series, Honda CB/CBR models, Suzuki SV650 and GSX-S, Kawasaki Ninja 650 and Z900, and Harley-Davidson fuel-injected Sportsters and Dyna models. The problem worsens in cold weather when the valve must open wider for cold idle.
causes: Carbon buildup on IACV pintle and bore from crankcase breather oil vapor
IACV stepper motor coil failure from heat exposure near cylinder head
Wiring connector corroded or intermittent from vibration
Throttle body bypass passage clogged with carbon restricting air to IACV
IACV calibration lost after battery disconnect — ECU must relearn idle position
fix_procedure: 1. Read DTCs: P0505 = IACV malfunction (general). P0506 = idle speed lower than expected (valve stuck closed). P0507 = idle speed higher than expected (valve stuck open). 2. Locate the IACV: usually bolted to the throttle body with 2-3 screws, connected by a wiring harness. It may be on the intake side or the airbox side depending on the model. 3. Remove the IACV and inspect the pintle: if it's coated in black carbon/oil film, that's the problem. 4. Clean the IACV: spray throttle body cleaner (NOT carb cleaner — it's too aggressive for the IACV seals) directly on the pintle and into the bore. Work the pintle in and out by hand (or by cycling power on 2-wire types). 5. Clean the throttle body bypass passage with a small brush and cleaner. 6. Reinstall the IACV with new gasket/O-ring. 7. Perform IACV relearn: warm the engine to operating temperature, turn off all accessories, let the engine idle for 5+ minutes. Some models require a specific key-on sequence (consult service manual). 8. Clear DTCs. 9. Forum tip: the IACV is one of the most misdiagnosed components on EFI motorcycles — mechanics replace the whole throttle body when all it needs is a 15-minute cleaning. On Yamaha FZ-09/MT-09 and FZ-07/MT-07, the IACV sticks at 15,000-20,000 miles like clockwork — clean it proactively during valve service. The Yamaha IACV is a 2-wire thermal type that's easy to clean in place. On Honda CB650R/CBR650R, the IACV is integrated into the throttle body — you clean the bypass passage directly. Kawasaki Z900 and Ninja 650 use a stepper motor IACV; if the stepper motor is failed (clicks but doesn't move), replacement is $80-120. On Harley Sportster EFI (2007-2021), the ISC is part of the throttle body assembly — cleaning requires removing the throttle body ($60 labor at a dealer, 20 minutes DIY). Suzuki SV650 (2017+) has a known idle surge issue at cold startup — 99% of the time it's the IACV needing cleaning, not a TPS or ECU issue.
parts_needed: Small nylon brush for bypass passage
IACV unit (if stepper motor is failed, model-specific)
Rags and gloves (cleaner is aggressive on skin)

title: Throttle body cleaning and synchronization — multi-cylinder EFI balance
description: EFI throttle bodies accumulate carbon deposits on the butterfly plates, bore walls, and idle bypass passages over time. On multi-cylinder engines, uneven carbon buildup causes throttle plates to seal differently, creating vacuum imbalance between cylinders. This produces rough idle, vibration at steady cruise, uneven exhaust note, and reduced power. Throttle body sync on EFI bikes is identical in principle to carb sync but uses electronic tools. This affects all multi-cylinder EFI motorcycles: Suzuki GSX-R600/750/1000, Honda CBR600RR/1000RR, Kawasaki ZX-6R/ZX-10R, Yamaha YZF-R1/R6, and Harley-Davidson V-twin models.
causes: Carbon buildup on throttle butterfly edges preventing full close/seal
Idle bypass passages in throttle body clogged with carbon and oil vapor
Throttle body sync screws drifted from engine vibration
Intake manifold O-rings or boots cracked causing unmetered air
Crankcase breather system depositing oil vapor on throttle plates
fix_procedure: 1. Remove the air filter/airbox to access the throttle body intake side. Inspect the butterfly plates and bore — black carbon buildup on the plate edges and bore walls is normal but needs cleaning when it affects sealing. 2. Clean with throttle body cleaner and a soft cloth. Do NOT use abrasive brushes on the bore — most throttle bodies have a factory coating that improves sealing. Spray cleaner on the cloth, wipe the butterfly edges and bore. Work the throttle open and closed to clean all surfaces. 3. Clean the idle bypass passages: spray cleaner into the small holes/ports around the throttle bore. Use compressed air to blow out dissolved carbon. 4. For multi-cylinder sync: connect a vacuum gauge set (TwinMax digital gauge for V-twins, Carbtune Pro or Morgan CarbTune for inline-4s) to the vacuum ports on each throttle body. 5. Warm the engine to operating temperature. Read vacuum on all cylinders at idle (1100-1300 RPM typical for EFI). Adjust sync screws between throttle bodies until all readings are within 1 cmHg. 6. On V-twins (Harley, SV650, V-Strom): there's one sync screw between the front and rear throttle body. Adjust until the TwinMax LED centers. 7. After sync: verify idle speed, check for DTCs, test ride. 8. Forum tip: throttle body cleaning and sync is the single best maintenance item for any EFI motorcycle with 15,000+ miles — it makes more difference than any fuel additive or ECU tune. The factory service interval is typically 24,000-26,000 miles (at valve service), but 12,000 miles is better for bikes ridden in dusty conditions or short-trip city riding. On Suzuki GSX-R models, the sync ports are sealed with rubber caps under the airbox — they're easy to miss. Honda CBR inline-4 throttle bodies have individual bypass screws per body; adjust each one independently before syncing. Kawasaki ZX models use a balance adapter harness (P/N 57001-1403, $40) to connect vacuum gauges. Harley V-twins with Delphi throttle bodies respond dramatically to cleaning — a surge or hunt at idle on a 2007-2017 Dyna or Sportster is almost always a dirty throttle body. The TwinMax digital gauge ($100) is the best tool for V-twin sync — mercury sticks work but are overkill for a 2-cylinder engine.
parts_needed: Vacuum gauge set (TwinMax for V-twin, Carbtune Pro for inline-4)
Intake manifold O-rings (inspect during service, replace if hardened)

title: Fuel pressure regulator failure — fuel rail pressure out of specification
description: The fuel pressure regulator maintains constant fuel rail pressure by returning excess fuel to the tank (return-style systems) or by controlling pump speed (returnless systems). A failed regulator causes fuel pressure to be too high (rich running, fuel smell, black spark plugs) or too low (lean running, hesitation, stalling under load). On vacuum-referenced regulators, a ruptured diaphragm allows fuel into the vacuum line and intake manifold. This affects Honda VFR800 and CBR series, Kawasaki Concours and ZX-14R, Yamaha FJR1300 and R1, Suzuki Hayabusa and Bandit, and Harley-Davidson Touring and Softail models.
causes: Regulator diaphragm ruptured — fuel leaks into vacuum line (return-style systems)
Regulator spring weakened from heat and vibration — pressure drops below spec
Regulator stuck closed — fuel pressure exceeds spec, causing rich running and injector damage
Fuel return line kinked or clogged — back-pressure mimics regulator failure
Returnless system pump driver module failed — pump runs at full speed constantly
fix_procedure: 1. Connect a fuel pressure gauge to the fuel rail test port. Key-on engine-off: pressure should reach system specification within 2-3 seconds (36-43 PSI Japanese, 55-62 PSI Harley). 2. Start the engine: pressure should remain stable at idle and rise slightly with throttle increase. If pressure drops steadily at idle: regulator leaking internally or fuel pump weak. 3. Check for regulator diaphragm failure (return-style systems): remove the vacuum line from the regulator. If fuel drips from the vacuum port, the diaphragm is ruptured — fuel is entering the intake manifold through the vacuum line. Replace immediately. 4. Pinch-test the fuel return line: briefly pinch the return line while reading fuel pressure. Pressure should rise to 80+ PSI (proving the pump is strong). If pressure doesn't rise: weak pump, not a regulator issue. 5. Replace the regulator: most are held by 2-3 bolts and a fuel line. Replace the O-rings. On return-style systems, verify the return line flows freely to the tank. 6. On returnless systems (many 2010+ models): the regulator is integrated into the fuel pump module. Replace the entire pump assembly. 7. Clear DTCs, verify pressure at idle and under load. 8. Forum tip: fuel pressure regulator failure is often misdiagnosed as a fuel pump issue because both cause similar symptoms (low power, stalling). The pinch-test in step 4 is the key diagnostic: if pressure rises when you pinch the return line, the pump is fine and the regulator is the problem. On Harley-Davidson Touring models (2008+), the fuel pressure regulator is integrated into the fuel pump module inside the tank — replacement means pulling the pump assembly ($250-400 OEM, $150-200 aftermarket). On Honda VFR800 (1998-2013), the regulator is external on the fuel rail and is a $45-60 part — easy 30-minute replacement. Kawasaki ZX-14R and Concours 1400 use a high-flow regulator that rarely fails but the fuel return line check valve sticks at 50K+ miles. Yamaha FJR1300 regulators are reliable but the vacuum reference hose deteriorates — inspect it during valve service. Suzuki Hayabusa (Gen 1) regulators fail at 40K+ miles; replace proactively with a Radium Engineering adjustable regulator ($120) for better fuel pressure stability.
parts_needed: Fuel pressure regulator (model-specific or universal adjustable)
Vacuum line for regulator reference (if applicable)
Fuel line clamps and hose for return line inspection

title: ECU/ECM reset and adaptation relearn — post-service idle and fueling restoration
description: The ECU (Engine Control Unit) on modern EFI motorcycles continuously adapts fuel trims, idle speed targets, and sensor offsets based on riding conditions and component wear. When the battery is disconnected, the ECU is flashed, or major service is performed (throttle body replacement, injector swap, TPS replacement), these learned adaptations are lost. The engine may idle erratically, run rich or lean, hesitate on tip-in, or throw false DTCs until the ECU relearns. This affects all EFI motorcycles but is most noticeable on Kawasaki ZX and Z series, Yamaha MT and R-series, Honda CBR and CB, Suzuki GSX-S and V-Strom, and Harley-Davidson models with adaptive fuel control.
causes: Battery disconnected during service — all adaptive memory cleared
ECU flash/reflash for performance tune or recall — adaptations reset to factory baseline
TPS or MAP sensor replaced — ECU learned offsets no longer match new sensor
Throttle body replaced or cleaned — idle air trim no longer matches physical airflow
Aftermarket fuel controller installed or removed — ECU base map confused by different fuel delivery
fix_procedure: 1. Understand what needs to relearn: after battery disconnect or ECU reset, the ECU needs to relearn idle trim (IACV position), short-term fuel trim (STFT), long-term fuel trim (LTFT), and TPS closed-throttle position. 2. TPS relearn (most makes): turn key ON, do NOT touch the throttle, wait 10 seconds. Turn key OFF, wait 10 seconds. Repeat 3 times. This lets the ECU sample closed-throttle TPS voltage. 3. Idle relearn: start the engine cold, let it idle without touching the throttle until the cooling fan cycles twice (or 15+ minutes). The ECU will hunt at first then stabilize as it learns the correct IACV position. 4. Fuel trim relearn: ride the bike through varied conditions — idle, city, highway, acceleration, deceleration — for 30-50 miles. The ECU needs to see the full operating range to set STFT and LTFT. 5. Verify with scan tool: STFT and LTFT should both be within +/- 10% after the relearn drive. If they're beyond +/- 15%, there's a mechanical issue (vacuum leak, injector problem) that the ECU is trying to compensate for. 6. Clear any DTCs that were set during the relearn period — they may be artifacts of the adaptation process, not real faults. 7. If idle remains erratic after a full relearn cycle: check for vacuum leaks, dirty IACV, or a TPS that needs calibration — the ECU can't adapt around a hardware fault. 8. Forum tip: ECU relearn is the most overlooked post-service step on EFI motorcycles. Mechanics disconnect the battery for 30 seconds to 'reset the ECU' as a diagnostic step, then wonder why the bike runs poorly for the next week. On Kawasaki ZX-6R/10R (2009+), the ECU adaptation is aggressive — it takes 50+ miles of varied riding to fully relearn. On Yamaha R1/R6 (2015+), the ECU has a specific dealer-mode relearn procedure using the YDS (Yamaha Diagnostic System) — the key-cycle method works but YDS is faster. Honda CBR models are forgiving — they typically relearn within 10 miles of riding. Suzuki GSX-R and GSX-S ECUs hold adaptive memory through brief battery disconnects (under 30 minutes) but lose it after longer disconnections. On Harley-Davidson (2014+ models with closed-loop O2), the relearn is critical after any exhaust or fuel system service — the ECU's adaptive fuel control (VE table) takes 100+ miles to fully stabilize. If a Harley customer complains the bike runs differently after service, check if the battery was disconnected — that's almost always the cause. The Screamin Eagle Pro Street Tuner ($400) or Dynojet Power Vision ($500) can force a full ECU adaptation reset and monitor the relearn process in real time.
parts_needed: OBD2/diagnostic scan tool (for DTC clearing and live data monitoring)
Battery charger/maintainer (to avoid another disconnect during relearn)
Service manual with model-specific relearn procedure
Dealer diagnostic tool (YDS, SDDS, KDS, or Digital Technician for make-specific procedures)
